primary_diagnosis.name: Acute spontaneous urticaria (likely post-viral), without angioedema or anaphylaxis

pathophysiology_summary: Acute urticaria is transient mast-cell and basophil degranulation in the superficial dermis releasing histamine and other mediators, producing pruritic weals with surrounding vasodilation (erythema) and central dermal oedema (the characteristic central pallor). Lesions are migratory and each individual weal resolves within 24 hours without scarring. In this case the likely driver is a recent viral upper-respiratory infection (a common non-allergenic trigger of acute spontaneous urticaria); no food, drug, or envenomation trigger was identified. The reaction is confined to skin/mucosa with no airway, respiratory, or circulatory involvement, so it does NOT meet criteria for anaphylaxis. The clinical concern is not the current presentation but the small possibility of progression to angioedema/anaphylaxis, which is managed by conservative treatment plus explicit safety-netting. Standard-dose H1 antihistamine failed; first-line escalation is up-dosing a non-sedating H1 antihistamine (guidelines support up to 4x standard dose), with a short course of oral corticosteroid for a severe acute flare.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden widespread intensely pruritic migratory raised weals — the defining pattern of urticaria.
Any acute widespread allergic-type skin eruption mandates an active screen for airway/respiratory/circulatory involvement before it can be excluded.
Deep dermal/mucosal swelling can accompany urticaria and threaten the airway if it involves lips/tongue/throat — must be screened for.
Considered but the eruption is generalised and migratory rather than localised to a contact site.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is urticaria, but anaphylaxis/angioedema must be held open until actively screened — the screen, not assumption, is what excludes them.

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory transient weals with central pallor, intense pruritus, recent viral illness, and a NEGATIVE airway/respiratory/circulatory screen — benign acute urticaria confirmed clinically.
No airway, respiratory, or circulatory involvement and no GI symptoms — does not meet anaphylaxis criteria. Remains the key safety-net contingency, not the current diagnosis.
Lesions are transient and migratory (resolving within 24h) with no fixed target lesions — argues strongly against erythema multiforme.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative red-flag screen is what downgrades anaphylaxis/angioedema from important-not-to-miss to a safety-net contingency and confirms benign acute urticaria.

differential.evidence_basis: Self-limiting acute urticaria; telehealth-manageable with antihistamine up-dosing, symptomatic measures, and conditional airway safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Angioedema of the lips, tongue or throat, or voice change — deep swelling threatening the upper airway
red_flags.clinical_significance: Upper-airway angioedema is the swelling that turns benign urticaria into an airway emergency; its absence on screening is what makes home management safe, and its appearance is the T5 escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to screen for and safety-net airway swelling risks a patient not recognising evolving angioedema/anaphylaxis and delaying a life-saving 000 call.

red_flags.description: Respiratory compromise — stridor, wheeze, chest tightness or difficulty breathing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Lower/upper airway involvement is a systemic (anaphylaxis) feature; its absence supports skin-limited urticaria.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised respiratory involvement is a missed anaphylaxis.

red_flags.description: Circulatory / systemic features — dizziness, faintness, collapse, or abdominal cramps (distributive shock)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Circulatory involvement would meet anaphylaxis criteria; its absence, with normal perfusion, confirms skin-limited disease.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed circulatory involvement is a missed anaphylaxis with risk of collapse.

red_flags.description: Individual weals persisting beyond 24 hours, painful or burning rather than itchy, or leaving bruising/pigmentation
red_flags.clinical_significance: These features suggest urticarial vasculitis rather than ordinary urticaria and warrant GP review and possible skin biopsy; the transient blanching migratory weals here argue against it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling urticarial vasculitis as simple urticaria delays investigation of an underlying systemic process.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being anchored by the explosive onset and the note's 'imminent anaphylaxis' framing and treating a skin-limited, haemodynamically stable urticaria as anaphylaxis — reaching for adrenaline and emergency escalation when no airway/respiratory/circulatory features are present.
Sending a stable patient with isolated urticaria to ED or calling 000 without any red flags — an over-triage that wastes resources, alarms the patient, and is a defect, not caution.

Correctly identifying benign urticaria but failing to give explicit, specific airway safety-netting (throat/tongue swelling, voice change, breathing difficulty -> call 000) — the one thing that must accompany reassurance.

Ordering allergy testing (specific IgE / skin prick), routine bloods, or serum tryptase in a first acute episode — none are indicated acutely and testing is only warranted if urticaria becomes chronic or recurrent.

Simply repeating a standard dose of a non-sedating antihistamine after it has already failed, rather than up-dosing (guidelines support up to 4x) and considering a short oral steroid course for a severe flare.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature and oxygen saturation (clinician-measured vitals)
Dermatographism elicited by firm stroking of the skin
Direct inspection of the oral mucosa, lips and tongue to formally exclude angioedema
Chest auscultation for air entry and wheeze
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None required at baseline — the patient is stable and skin-limited. Manage with antihistamine up-dosing and symptomatic care via telehealth, and give explicit conditional safety-netting: call 000 immediately for any lip/tongue/throat swelling, voice change, difficulty breathing or swallowing, or faintness.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the urticaria diagnosis (pattern is characteristic). The real risks are the opposite errors: OVER-action (treating as anaphylaxis / over-triaging a stable patient) and UNDER-safety-netting (reassuring without a clear airway escalation threshold).

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Second-generation (non-sedating) H1 antihistamine is first-line; up-dose to as much as 4x the standard dose before adding other agents. Sedating antihistamines are not recommended first-line.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCIA Urticaria / Acute Allergic Reaction guidance
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Acute urticaria without angioedema or systemic features is not anaphylaxis; adrenaline is not indicated. Provide clear safety-netting for progression to airway swelling or anaphylaxis.
